title: Doctors brace for Medicare fraud blitz
full_text:
BUDGET 2026: HEALTH Doctors’ billing practices will be scrutinised more heavily than ever as the federal government moves to establish expanded Medicare compliance capabilities with the aim of clawing back $230m every year from fraud detection.
The initiative that initially aims to reap $674.4m over four years through expanded Medicare compliance was buried in budget papers that championed Labor’s ongoing commitment to universal healthcare.
Labor has committed to funding Medicare Urgent Care Clinics on a permanent basis at a cost of $580.2m a year, and moved to directly fund – at a cost of $25.3bn – six bulk-billing GP clinics in the Hunter and Central Coast regions of NSW where people cannot access bulk-billing services.
There’s also a $6.5bn spend on delivering cheaper medicines, including a host of new drugs on the Pharmaceutical Benefits Scheme.
Adult dental services for low-income individuals and those on concession cards have been funded permanently for the first time. The Public Dental Services for Adults agreement will deliver commonwealth funds to the states to administer dental services for eligible patients at a cost of $107.8m a year.
This year’s budget does not contain any significant new big-spending initiatives in health and instead aims to bed down the big initiatives Labor took to voters when it was elected – expanding bulk billing, urgent care centres and cheaper medicines.
Health Minister Mark Butler has said that cracking down on specialist fees will be a big focus of the government’s second-term agenda. Plans are under way to publish this year the median fees of all specialists on a public website in a move that is sparking deep concern among medical specialists and medical organisations on the basis of accuracy, even though doctors’ groups generally support greater transparency.
The move is just one of the fronts on which the traditional closed-shop structures of the medical profession are being challenged by government. The unprecedented crackdown on Medicare fraud will emerge as another controversial initiative given the recent history of Medicare compliance, which has not always operated fairly toward doctors.
The independent Review of Medicare Integrity and Compliance, commissioned by the government three years ago, found there was a huge amount of wastage from Medicare in the order of $1.5bn to $3bn a year. Most of that was attributed to byzantine processes and billing errors rather than deliberate fraud by doctors.
The review, authored by Deloitte’s Pradeep Philip, found that Medicare compliance systems often punished doctors who were trying to do the right thing. That’s why setting a savings target from fraud detection – especially one as large as $230m a year – is eye-opening. Doctors will be concerned that those who do not intend to bill fraudulently may be caught up in the compliance net.
The government says its compliance spend implements the recommendations of the Pradeep review. Part of that review also emphasised that Medicare’s systems were critically fragmented, disjointed and not fit for purpose. The government has moved to address this monumental issue. It is spending $745.1m over four years from 2026-27 to “strengthen the integrity and functionality of critical Medicare systems and infrastructure” to provide greater efficiency and system effectiveness. The spend includes enhancement of My Health Record to pave the way for sharing-by-default arrangements concerning patients’ health information. It is designed to reap $146.3m in savings over two years.
summary: The Australian government plans to enhance Medicare compliance to recover $230 million annually from fraud, while also funding various health initiatives including permanent Medicare Urgent Care Clinics and adult dental services.
author: NATASHA ROBINSON, HEALTH
heading: Health and Disability Insurance
key_points: Expanded Medicare compliance aims to recover $674.4 million over four years.
Funding for permanent Medicare Urgent Care Clinics and adult dental services introduced.
Concerns raised about the impact of compliance measures on doctors not engaging in fraud.
relevance: This initiative highlights the financial implications of healthcare compliance, which may affect actuarial assessments and risk management in the health sector.
